stops.name: Odeon of Herodes Atticus
stops.shortDescription: A magnificent stone Roman theater structure completed in 161 AD on the southwest slope of the Acropolis. Restored in the 1950s, it still actively hosts world-class musical and theatrical performances under the stars.

stops.name: Parthenon
stops.shortDescription: The crown jewel of the Acropolis, dedicated to the goddess Athena and completed in 438 BC. It represents the zenith of the Doric order and is an enduring symbol of Ancient Greece and democracy.

stops.name: Erechtheion
stops.shortDescription: An ancient Greek temple famous for its elegant Porch of the Caryatids, where six sculpted female figures serve as supporting columns. Legend states this is the exact spot where Athena and Poseidon battled for patronage of the city.

stops.name: Areopagus Hill
stops.shortDescription: A prominent rock outcropping that served as the ancient high court for deliberate homicide. It is also known as Mars Hill, where the Apostle Paul famously delivered a sermon to the Athenians.

stops.name: Ancient Agora of Athens
stops.shortDescription: The commercial, political, and social heart of ancient Athens. This is the very marketplace where philosophers like Socrates engaged citizens in debate and the concept of democracy was born.

stops.name: Temple of Hephaestus
stops.shortDescription: Perched atop Agoraios Kolonos hill, this is the most well-preserved ancient Greek temple in the world. Dedicated to the god of craftsmanship, it remained largely intact because it was converted into a Christian church in the 7th century.

stops.name: Stoa of Attalos
stops.shortDescription: An impressive covered walkway in the Ancient Agora. Completely reconstructed in the 1950s, it now houses the Museum of the Ancient Agora, displaying artifacts found directly on the site.

stops.name: Hadrian's Library
stops.shortDescription: Created by Roman Emperor Hadrian in 132 AD to house his massive collection of papyrus scrolls. It originally featured a beautiful central courtyard with a large pool and reading rooms.

stops.name: Monastiraki Square
stops.shortDescription: A vibrant, bustling square seamlessly blending multiple eras of Athenian history. It offers incredible views up to the Acropolis and is the gateway to a lively, sprawling flea market.

stops.name: Roman Agora
stops.shortDescription: The commercial center built during the Roman period with donations from Julius Caesar and Augustus. It served as the primary marketplace after the original Ancient Agora became overcrowded.

stops.name: Tower of the Winds
stops.shortDescription: Often considered the world's first meteorological station. This octagonal Pentelic marble tower functioned simultaneously as a massive sundial, a water clock, and a wind vane in antiquity.

stops.name: Plaka District
stops.shortDescription: Known affectionately as the 'Neighborhood of the Gods,' this is Athens' oldest historical neighborhood. Its narrow, winding streets are lined with neoclassical architecture, cafes, and bright bougainvillea.

stops.name: Arch of Hadrian
stops.shortDescription: A monumental marble gateway constructed in 131 AD. Its famous inscriptions playfully divide the city into the old 'City of Theseus' on one side and the new 'City of Hadrian' on the other.

stops.name: Temple of Olympian Zeus
stops.shortDescription: A colossal ruined temple dedicated to the king of the Olympian gods. Although only 15 of its original 104 massive Corinthian columns remain standing today, their sheer scale is awe-inspiring.
